articles.title: Astronomers Discover an Exoplanet in the Wrong Place, Hinting at a Planetary System That Was Born 'Inside Out'
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Astronomers Discover an Exoplanet in the Wrong Place, Hinting at a Planetary System That Was Born ‘Inside Out’
A rocky planet was found orbiting its host star in an unusual location
Planetary systems...
articles.content:
Astronomers Discover an Exoplanet in the Wrong Place, Hinting at a Planetary System That Was Born ‘Inside Out’
A rocky planet was found orbiting its host star in an unusual location
Planetary systems in the Milky Way galaxy tend to follow a particular pattern: rocky planets toward the center, closest to the host star, and gas planets toward the exterior. That’s how planets are organized around our sun, with rocky Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars first in the lineup, followed by the gaseous Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune.
But astronomers have found a rule breaker. The cool and faint red dwarf star LHS 1903, some 116 light-years away, hosts four planets that clearly don’t care for convention, since a rocky planet somehow seems to orbit at the outer edge, boxing in two gas planets and another rocky planet that lies closest to the star. The findings, published February 12 in the journal Science, might hint at an unusual mechanism behind the birth of some planets.
“[The LHS 1903 system] follows that [standard] pattern beautifully for the first three planets,” study co-author Andrew Cameron, an astronomer at the University of St. Andrews in Scotland, tells Science News’ Adam Mann. “Then, something weird happened to the fourth planet.”
Computer simulations typically indicate that a planetary system’s innermost members are rocky because the host star’s energy blasts away their atmospheres. Gas giants, on the other hand, tend to take shape at a greater distance, in colder areas where gas can build up.
Cameron and his colleagues found the outlier while looking at data collected from 2019 to 2023 by NASA’s Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS), which searches distant stars for signs of orbiting planets. That revealed a planetary system around the star LHS 190, which is roughly half the sun’s mass. Follow-up observations with other telescopes unveiled some properties of the four planets, including size, mass and density, which hinted at their compositions and unusual order.
So, what’s up with the rocky weirdo in the wrong place? After ruling out some possible explanations, computer simulations hint that the outermost planet, called LHS 1903 e, may have been born without an atmosphere. The planetary system may have followed an “inside-out” formation mechanism, in which the inner planets came together before the outer ones—the opposite way that the solar system’s planets are thought to have emerged.
“[That] means the outermost planet formed millions of years after the innermost one. And because [LHS 1903 e] formed later, there was actually not that much gas and dust in the disk left to build this planet from,” study co-author Thomas Wilson, a planetary astrophysicist at the University of Warwick in England, tells CNN’s Jacopo Prisco.
The out-of-place planet could be an astronomical weirdo, or it may offer a first glimpse at a previously unobserved planetary system dynamic, per a statement.
Fun fact: How many exoplanets have we found?
Last year, NASA’s official tally reached 6,000 exoplanets. Still, more than 8,000 candidates were awaiting confirmation at that time.
“The team that conducted this work consists of experts at the top of the field, and they have done a very fine job with the data,” says Lauren Weiss, an astrophysicist at the University of Notre Dame who wasn’t involved in the new study, to Scientific American’s Jacek Krywko. “As for their conclusion that LHS 1903 e formed in a gas-depleted environment, I would have liked to see a more detailed experiment exploring the giant-impact scenario,” she adds. The study authors doubt that the rocky planet started with gas and lost it via some dramatic collision.
Néstor Espinoza, an astronomer at the Space Telescope Science Institute who was not involved in the work, tells CNN that the discovery will help researchers better understand the birth of planets, especially around tiny stars like LHS 1903. “This system adds a very interesting data point that will have planet formation models trying to explain it for years to come—and I’m sure we will learn something new about the process of planet formation once they are compared against each other,” he writes in an email.
